---
title: "A Directory of Canadian Metaresearch Initiatives"
subtitle: "Canadian Metaresearch Data Challenge — 2nd Canadian Open Science Conference"
author: "Zachary Batist, McGill University"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  pdf:
      documentclass: article
      geometry: margin=0.75in
      fontsize: 11pt
      toc: false
      header-includes: |
        \usepackage{fancyhdr}
        \pagestyle{fancy}
        \usepackage{lastpage}
        \fancyhf{}
        \fancyhead[L]{A Directory of Canadian Metaresearch Initiatives}
        \fancyhead[R]{Zachary Batist, McGill University}
        \fancyfoot[R]{\thepage\ / \pageref*{LastPage}}
        \renewcommand{\headrulewidth}{0.4pt}
        \AtBeginDocument{\thispagestyle{fancy}}
        \usepackage{etoolbox}
        \makeatletter
        \patchcmd{\maketitle}{\newpage}{}{}{}
        \renewcommand{\maketitle}{}
        \makeatother
---

## Objectives

Metaresearch in Canada is practiced under different banners, including metascience, scientometrics, science and technology studies, and science reform advocacy, by people working across universities, libraries, granting agencies, and independent community infrastructure projects. Much of this work happens through working groups, community infrastructure projects, collaborative platforms, and institutional venues that bring people and resources together around a shared problem or method. No prior effort has systematically documented these initiatives in Canada, or the people and publications connected to them.

This project builds a directory of Canadian metaresearch initiatives, the people who contribute to them, and the publications that result. It draws on experience building and maintaining [open-archaeo.info](https://open-archaeo.info/), a comprehensive listing of open source research software in archaeology used as a data resource to track developments in that field, and on technical work retrieving, processing, and analyzing aggregated data from scholarly metadata resources.

## Methodology and Data Model

The directory comprises three linked data files: initiatives, people, and publications, stored as JSON arrays and connected by overlapping keys. A detailed technical account of the data model, sources, and processing pipeline is maintained at [zackbatist.info/CMDC](https://zackbatist.info/CMDC).

Initiatives are identified from three sources, each retrieved and processed differently. Canadian-affiliated OpenAlex works are queried by institution and by metascience-specific topic, obtained from OpenAlex's own topic hierarchy; where a result has an open-access link, the full text is processed with GROBID to extract its acknowledgements section, checked against a bilingual term list, since an initiative is often named there in passing rather than as the paper's subject. CIHR, NSERC, SSHRC, and FRQ funding data are searched by matching the same term list against project titles and descriptions, using bulk downloads where available. Research Data Canada and the Digital Research Alliance of Canada's own published working-group listings are read in full.

Candidate names from all three are normalized and deduplicated through string matching and shared-person or shared-institution overlap. A candidate is confirmed if it has its own documentation or is independently named in at least two sources; everything else is retained on a published, unconfirmed list.

Confirmed initiatives are enriched with additional detail drawn from their own source material and cross-referenced identifiers: institutional affiliation and any stated funding relationship from the source that surfaced the initiative, membership names cross-checked against ORCID and OpenAlex author identifiers to disambiguate people who share a name, and a plain-language description built from the initiative's own documentation where it exists. Where an initiative has no independent documentation of its own, only what a funding record or working-group listing states about it is retained, rather than inferring detail that isn't actually stated anywhere.

People records are seeded from the people a confirmed initiative names as members or contributors, then matched against ORCID and OpenAlex author identifiers to add institutional affiliation history and self-described research interests. A person's own account of what they work on and what a bibliometric record shows they have published capture different things; where they diverge, that divergence is preserved as data.

Publication records, structured as a custom extension of BibLaTeX, are retrieved from OpenAlex and Crossref for each confirmed initiative and its people, supplemented by GROBID-extracted metadata where source metadata is incomplete, and tagged using a working vocabulary from a recent paper on metascience [@rubin2026sixtypes].

This method is expected to miss initiatives with no online presence, informal groups with no searchable documentation, and work in languages or communities the source and term lists don't reach. A dated register of known gaps is published alongside the unconfirmed list.

## Anticipated Applications and Impact

As a web directory and discovery tool, the dataset lets someone find the initiatives, people, and publications connected to a given problem or method, including contributions that never took the form of a citable output. As a dataset, it supports analysis of the community's diversity, capturing activity that publication records alone tend to miss. As a platform for advocacy, it gives a concrete, browsable picture of a distributed research community, relevant to ongoing calls, including the federal science advisor's data-sharing strategy, to develop infrastructure that connects Canada's distributed research efforts.

Beyond the initial release, the directory could function as a living, updatable record for Canadian metaresearch networks such as CARN/RECAR, supporting knowledge mobilization: identifying which initiatives are working on adjacent problems without knowing it, who to invite into a conversation, or where a funder's or institution's own metaresearch capacity actually sits.

## Openness, Transparency, and Reproducibility

Retrieval queries, inclusion and exclusion criteria, disambiguation procedures, and the tag-vocabulary mapping are published as versioned, dated artifacts in a public repository, alongside the retrieval, enrichment, and analysis code. The dataset is openly licensed. Contact information collected for internal disambiguation is not included in the public release. Full-text-derived fields are included only where source licensing permits redistribution; where it does not, only derived tags are published.

## Feasibility and Work Plan

Assuming notification on August 1, 2026 against an October 27 to 29, 2026 conference date, roughly twelve weeks are available. Initiative discovery and confirmation across the four sources, weeks 1 through 4, come first, since the people and publication layers depend on confirmed initiatives. Person and publication retrieval and disambiguation, weeks 4 through 9, are the largest share of the remaining work, using the same OpenAlex and ORCID-based disambiguation approach used in the author's other current work reconstructing citation networks (BibVik). Validation and the gaps register, weeks 9 through 10, depend on the first two phases being substantially complete. Public release and documentation, weeks 10 through 12, depend on validation being complete. Deeper analysis beyond initiative membership, including funding relationships and governance structures, is treated as future work.
